what_i_noticed: The 3-state Gaussian HMM (backend/models/hmm.py) existed with full fitting logic but was never called by any MC path. The MC engine had dead hmm_drift_blend=0.15 and hmm_vol_blend=0.15 parameters with complete blending code in simulate_paths(), but they were always None. Additionally, hmm.py had hardcoded constants (500, 200, 5, 20), a broad except Exception block, and used no config. drift_detector.py had a reproducibility bug using np.random.uniform instead of seeded RNG.
hypothesis: Wiring the existing 3-state Gaussian HMM into all Monte Carlo call sites will activate the dead hmm_drift_blend/hmm_vol_blend parameters, producing regime-conditioned simulations with probabilistic drift/vol blending instead of the current rule-based binary regime assignments.
what_i_did: 1. Added fit_hmm_for_mc() bridge function in regime_detector.py that calls fit_hmm_regimes() and returns MC-ready dict with state_means/state_vols/regime_probs. 2. Wired HMM into all 3 MC call sites: SP500 projection (simulation.py), per-stock MC (stock.py + stock_analyzer.py), sector MC (sector.py + sector_analyzer.py). 3. Added HMM config section to config.py with all formerly hardcoded values. 4. Narrowed except blocks in hmm.py and regime_detector.py. 5. Fixed drift_detector.py reproducibility bug (np.random.uniform -> self._rng.uniform). 6. Wrote 37 new tests covering HMM fitting, bridge function, MC integration, config, drift detector, and signature passthrough.
analysis: This was a high-impact, low-risk wiring change. The MC engine already had full HMM blending logic — it just never received inputs. The bridge function pattern (fit_hmm_for_mc returning a dict) keeps the HMM model decoupled from the MC engine. Graceful fallback means zero regression risk: if HMM fitting fails, None values flow through and MC operates exactly as before. The 15% blend weight is conservative enough that HMM won't dominate, but provides meaningful regime conditioning. The drift_detector reproducibility fix was a genuine bug — KS test results varied across identical inputs due to legacy np.random global state. Config extraction from hmm.py eliminates 6 hardcoded values. The narrowed except blocks now catch only expected failure modes (convergence failures, singular matrices, missing keys) instead of swallowing everything.
what_i_would_do_differently: 1. Should have measured the actual impact on MC output distributions before vs after HMM wiring — e.g., run simulate_paths with and without HMM on real market data and compare percentile spreads, not just synthetic tests. 2. The HMM is fitted from scratch on every API call (not cached). For production, fitting once per cache cycle would be much more efficient. 3. The 15% blend weight is inherited from existing config — should validate whether this is too low or too high by comparing HMM-blended simulations against historical realized returns. 4. Could have added a test that verifies HMM regime labels roughly match the rule-based regime detector on known historical periods (e.g., COVID crash should be Crisis/Bear).
next_steps: 1. Cache HMM fit results with same TTL as simulation cache (1hr) to avoid re-fitting on every request. 2. Validate HMM blend weight (15%) against historical backtests — compare MC percentile accuracy with/without HMM. 3. Add transition matrix utilization — currently only using current regime probs, not forward-looking transition dynamics. 4. Investigate risk_scorer.py broad except blocks (3 identified in exploration, not addressed this cycle).
